1: theme Blessed is the one who considers the poor! In the day of trouble the LORD delivers him. The beatitude on care for the poor reaches its fulfillment in Christ who pronounces himself the poor to be cared for: I was sick and you visited me... as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me (Matthew 25:36-40). The one who delivers the poor is also the one who identifies with them.
2: theme The LORD protects him and keeps him alive; he is called blessed in the land. Christ is both the Lord who protects and the one who is called blessed: Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! (Luke 19:38). The one who guards the life of the one who considers the poor is himself the one who laid down his life for the poor in spirit.
3: shadow The LORD sustains him on his sickbed; in his illness you restore him to full health. Christ the healer restores full health by bearing sickness: He took our illnesses and bore our diseases (Matthew 8:17, citing Isaiah 53:4). The one who restores others to health was himself struck down; the healing flows from his wounds.
4: shadow As for me, I said, O LORD, be gracious to me; heal me, for I have sinned against you! Christ who knew no sin became sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21). Though he had nothing to confess, he descended into the sinner's place — the one who is grace incarnate (John 1:14) receives the petition for healing on behalf of all who pray through him (Romans 8:34).
5: shadow My enemies say of me in malice, When will he die, and his name perish? Christ's enemies plot his death and the erasure of his name. But the name above every name (Philippians 2:9) cannot perish — it is the name at which every knee bows. The malice directed at the righteous sufferer only serves to advance the plan by which his name is exalted forever.
6: shadow And when one comes to see me, he utters empty words, while his heart gathers iniquity; when he goes out, he tells it abroad. The hypocrisy of those who came to Jesus with empty words while plotting his death: the Pharisees who tested him (Matthew 22:15-22), the Sanhedrin members who interrogated him. Their false concern gathers iniquity against the righteous one.
7: shadow All who hate me whisper together against me; they imagine the worst for me. The Sanhedrin conspiracy: The chief priests and the elders of the people gathered in the palace of the high priest and plotted together (Matthew 26:3-4). The whispering conspiracy against Christ fulfills this pattern of enmity toward the righteous sufferer.
8: shadow They say, A deadly thing is poured out on him; he will not rise again from where he lies. The fatal word — he will not rise again — is the very thing the resurrection overturns. Those who sealed the tomb (Matthew 27:66) intended to make the prophecy of enemies permanent. The resurrection of Christ is the definitive refutation of every voice that says he will not rise.
9: direct Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me. John 13:18 quotes this verse explicitly as Jesus identifies his betrayer at the Last Supper: the Scripture will be fulfilled: He who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me. The betrayal by Judas — who had eaten with Jesus — is the precise fulfillment of Psalms 41:9.
10: shadow But you, O LORD, be gracious to me, and raise me up, that I may repay them! The prayer for gracious raising — raise me up — is the resurrection prayer. God is gracious to Christ and raises him up, not to repay enemies with vengeance but to intercede for them (Luke 23:34; Romans 8:34).
11: shadow By this I know that you delight in me: my enemy will not shout in triumph over me. The Father's delight in the Son is declared at the Baptism and Transfiguration: This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased (Matthew 3:17; Psalms 17:5). The enemy's shout at the cross is temporary; the resurrection silences the triumph and confirms the Father's delight.
12: shadow But you have upheld me because of my integrity, and set me in your presence forever. God upholds Christ because of his perfect integrity — the sinless Son (Hebrews 4:15; 1 Peter 2:22) — and sets him in his presence forever: he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high (Hebrews 1:3). The eternal session of Christ fulfills this promise of being set in God's presence.
13: theme Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting! Amen and Amen. The doxology closing Book I of the Psalter. Christ himself is the eternal Yes and Amen to every promise of God (2 Corinthians 1:20 — all the promises of God find their Yes in him). The double Amen echoes through the Book of Revelation (Revelation 22:20).
